data.title: 'Housing First' became housing only
data.description:
Americans know something is broken across our communities, and particularly in our major cities.

Across the country, Americans see encampments growing, addiction and severe mental illness playing out in public, and vulnerable Americans trapped in cycles of instability and despair.

Americans are compassionate and they want to solve this problem. At the Department of Housing and Urban Development, we have been entrusted to find and fund the most effective solutions.

For more than a decade, homelessness policy centered on what is called “Housing First” — the belief that permanent government housing without any requirements or preconditions could solve homelessness. What began as a well-intentioned effort became an orthodoxy that treated free housing as the answer, even when the underlying causes, such as addiction, severe mental illness, or job loss, remained unaddressed.

In 2013, the department began mandating adherence to this orthodoxy. Since then, unsheltered homelessness has increased 36 percent and chronic homelessness has increased 80.5 percent, even as taxpayer-funded housing for the homeless has increased 151 percent.

Stable housing matters, but government housing alone is not recovery. Too often, Housing First became Housing Only. At its inception, Housing First had promised an end to homelessness within 10 years. We now know that this was a false promise.

Despite years of record federal spending, homelessness in America has continued to rise. Billions of taxpayer dollars have been poured into permanent government housing while scant attention was paid to treatment, recovery, and long-term self-sufficiency. Starting in 2020, the department’s spending on the Continuum of Care program surged by more than 50 percent, yet street homelessness rose by more than 20 percent by the end of the Biden administration.

Cities such as Los Angeles, San Francisco, Portland, Seattle and New York spent billions expanding homelessness programs, yet they still saw rising unsheltered homelessness, public disorder and addiction crises. Americans now increasingly question whether the system expected too little of itself and the people it was meant to help.

The people working within homelessness services are compassionate and committed, and many programs produced real results. But the Housing First model increasingly measured success by units filled instead of lives changed. Over time, it fueled a homeless-industrial complex more focused on keeping units filled and money flowing to those who provide services, rather than achieving recovery and self-sufficiency.

The original goal of the Continuum of Care program was to provide a continuum of effective services that addressed all factors involved in homelessness. It was never intended to be a one-size-fits-all-approach.

Housing will not solve a drug crisis. A set of keys cannot by itself treat severe mental illness, addiction or trauma. Yet policymakers act as though permanent government housing alone could substitute for treatment, recovery and accountability.

In practice, citizens were forced to accept public disorder and suffering, so long as someone had been given house keys. In some cases, the same cities that spend untold billions on homelessness services also encouraged the activities that lead to homelessness — for example, legalizing drug use, not enforcing drug laws, giving booze to the homeless and providing drug paraphernalia, including through needle exchange sites. These cities then act surprised when homelessness becomes worse than ever, and demand more money to address a problem whose causes they themselves are fueling.

We have visited faith-based ministries, shelters, and recovery programs across the country, including in Los Angeles, Austin and Baltimore, where people who once struggled with homelessness are now working, rebuilding relationships and living independently. These programs succeed because they treat the whole person, not just housing needs.

Expanding the continuum of solutions will include faith-based organizations as full partners in this work. Long before government built modern homelessness systems, ministries and recovery groups helped vulnerable Americans find community, accountability and hope. And the faith-based community is poised to fill this role once again.

We have seen firsthand that people struggling with homelessness need more than a free apartment; they need treatment, stability, purpose, and the chance to rebuild.

That is why the Trump administration is pursuing a different path and leaving the failed Housing First model behind.

Our department is restoring the Continuum of Care program — the federal government’s largest homelessness assistance program — to its focus on outcomes, accountability and self-sufficiency. The upcoming 2026 Continuum of Care Notice of Funding Opportunity will prioritize programs that restore a balanced approach and demonstrate measurable results in treatment, employment and long-term stability.

Success cannot be measured by housing units filled or dollars spent. We must move from funding programs to funding results.

Transitional housing, treatment, workforce development, and local programs that help people regain control of their lives are providing real results. Implementing President Trump’s executive order “Ending Crime and Disorder on America’s Streets” alongside the Department of Housing and Urban Development’s federal partners will ensure that federal resources support recovery and public order rather than prolonged dependency.

We have met people across this country who want to earn another chance — not a lifetime of government handouts. Compassion should restore dignity and encourage self-sufficiency, rather than indefinite dependency.

I am proud of the prograss we have made so far at the Department of Housing and Urban Development in changing our nation’s mindset toward homelessness. I look forward to helping more and more of our fellow citizens return to the path to self-sufficiency and independence, so that they can live out their own American Dream.

Scott Turner is the 19th secretary of Housing and Urban Development.

data.title: Ohio data center tax break suspended amid battle over paying cost for AI power
data.description:
Ohio Gov. Mike DeWine (R) suspended the state’s tax exemption for new data centers, which has drawn developers of the power facilities to the Buckeye State amid the boom of artificial intelligence.

DeWine, in an announcement Wednesday, said he directed the chair of the Ohio Tax Credit Authority to temporarily halt consideration of new data center tax exemption requests while the Ohio General Assembly “studies the growth” of data centers in the state.

Ohio joins a handful of other states considering changes to its tax breaks on data centers as technology companies are increasingly asked to bear the costs of the large-scale facilities.

Ohio’s tax breaks for data centers increased to $1.5 billion last year, far over the $135 million projections, according to local news reports.

“The Legislature removed this Sales Tax Exemption on Data Centers in the 2025 budget, but that was vetoed,” Ohio state Rep. David Thomas (R) wrote on X following DeWine’s announcement. “One year later, what was estimated at $300 million in lost revenue is now $1.6 billion. The legislature was not told of this cost until seeing it in the newspaper.”

DeWine said he “fully supports” the Ohio Legislature’s “work to study the issue and bring forward facts about data centers, including the local benefits to communities when tax exemptions are granted.”

Backlash over AI and the data centers used to power its development has increased in the past year, with concerns emerging over voters’ utility bills and the facilities’ impact on local communities and the environment. The tech industry’s expansion of the centers is becoming a wedge issue in this year’s midterms and state budget fights.

Some local and state governments have proposed moratoriums on data centers as a way to pause their growth. DeWine emphasized this week’s announcement does not do this, calling data centers “a critical component to today’s technology-driven economy.”

Local groups in Ohio are trying to get a data center ban on the November ballot that would prohibit data centers with a peak load of more than 25 megawatts per month. They will need to get more than 413,000 signatures from at least 44 of the 88 Ohio counties by July 1, the Ohio Capital Journal reported.

data.title: Biden adviser: Iran deal 'may be the best of the very bad outcomes'
data.description:
Former national security adviser Jake Sullivan on Friday blasted the Trump administration’s approach to negotiating with Iran but said the tentative deal that U.S. officials are considering may be their best option left.

Sullivan, who helped negotiate the Obama administration’s 2015 Iran nuclear deal, said in an interview on MS NOW’s “Morning Joe” that he thinks President Trump is looking seriously at the deal because “he does not have a good alternative.”

“He tried to bomb Iran, he tried to blockade Iran, he tried to bully Iran, and he is stuck,” Sullivan said. “So, this may be the best of the very bad outcomes available to him, and it just shows you what a misbegotten war this was from the beginning.”

The tentative deal, first reported by Axios on Thursday, would be a 60-day memorandum of understanding (MOU) to extend the fragile ceasefire and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, which would begin to relieve the enormous economic pressure facing both Washington and Tehran.  

Iran would agree not to impose tolls on commercial ships trying to cross the key waterway, through which more than 20 percent of the world’s oil and gas supply passes, and to remove all mines from the strait.  

In exchange, the U.S. would lift its naval blockade and issue some sanctions waivers so Iran could freely sell its oil. The U.S. would also commit to discussing broader sanction relief. It’s unclear if some Iranian funds would be unfrozen as part of the initial deal or left for later talks.  

“This is the umpteenth time we’ve heard we’re very close to a deal, but every time we hear this, the terms get a little better for Iran,” Sullivan said, calling the administration’s months-long effort in Iran “a huge strategic setback for the United States, a significant gain for Iran.”

The MOU would also include a commitment from Iran not to pursue a nuclear weapon, but Tehran has made similar assertions for years that have been disputed by nonproliferation experts. The first issues up for negotiation would be how to dispose of Iran’s highly enriched uranium and how to address future enrichment, Axios reported. 

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told reporters on Thursday that no concessions from the U.S. would be considered until Iran agrees to open the strait and give up any nuclear ambitions.

“Nothing is going to be on the table until we see the Strait of Hormuz open and the Iranians agree that they have to turn over the highly enriched uranium,” he said during a press briefing Thursday.

data.title: This is not the time to withdraw troops from Europe
data.description:
On May 19, the Russian Defense Ministry announced the commencement of a major exercise consisting of 64,000 personnel and 7,800 units, “including more than 200 missile launchers, more than 140 aircraft, 73 surface ships and 13 submarines, including 8 strategic missile submarines.” The ministry added that “during the exercise, the issues of joint training and use of nuclear weapons deployed on the territory of the Republic of Belarus will be worked out.” 

The announcement caught observers by surprise. Neither the defense nor the foreign ministry had provided forewarning of the impending exercise as has usually been the case. And this exercise was the largest in years.

The exercise included the dual capable Oreshnik intermediate range ballistic missile that Russia deployed to Belarus for the first time last year.

Three days after the exercise ended, Russia fired a non-nuclear Oreshnik that hit the Ukrainian city of Bila Tserkva near Kyiv. The missile was part of a massive attack that included 600 strike drones and 90 missiles launched from air, sea and land.

It was the third time during the war that Russia had fired an Oreshnik at Ukraine and the first time since January.

The timing of the exercise and the attack is unlikely to have been coincidental. Both were almost certainly intended to send not only a message to Ukraine, but also to NATO, that Moscow has no intention of backing away from its wartime objectives.

Nevertheless, while Putin may not be losing his war in Ukraine, he is certainly not winning it. Now in its fifth year, the conflict shows no sign of ending any time soon, and for the first time in two years, Ukraine is winning back territory from Russian forces. Indeed, Brigadier General Andriy Biletsky, commander of Ukraine’s Third Army Corps, asserted earlier this week that Russian forces were exhausted and that Kyiv was about to achieve a turning point in the war.

In light of Moscow’s setbacks on the battlefield, both the Russian nuclear exercise and the latest drone and missile attack reflect Putin’s growing frustration with the course the war appears to be taking.

Whether Putin will conclude that he can only gain the upper hand in the war if Russia fires a nuclear tipped Oreshnik or some other nuclear weapon is uncertain, but cannot be ruled out. He has previously threatened to employ nuclear weapons in the war. This apparently intimidated the Biden administration, which responded by delaying the shipment to Kyiv of weapons the Ukrainians desperately needed. 

Putin could also express his frustration by shifting his focus to the Baltic states, at which Russia continues to fire drones. Indeed, there is a growing fear in Europe that, faced with what is at best a stalemate in Ukraine, Putin will order an attack against one of Russia’s small neighbors. 

Russia’s forces are stretched thin; Moscow has lost a half million troops with hundreds of thousands more wounded. Nevertheless, Russia certainly has the wherewithal to launch an attack on one of the three small Baltic states should it choose to do so. That the Trump administration reportedly has discussed the possibility of withdrawing most of its forces from Europe only adds to concerns that Russia might launch such an offensive if the war in Ukraine continues to go badly.

President Trump himself has not been specific about which troops permanently deployed in Europe would actually be brought home. Moreover, he has stated that 5,000 additional troops would be deployed to Poland, presumably from Germany. This would certainly reinforce NATO’s deterrent against any Russian plans to attack one of its members. 

Washington continues to be preoccupied with the Iranian closure of the Strait of Hormuz, its ongoing skirmishes with Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps forces and its efforts to contain Tehran’s nuclear ambitions. Its efforts to negotiate a ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine have faltered. Nevertheless, it must respond to the Kremlin’s latest moves. It has to dissuade Moscow from attempting any action against a NATO state, which would trigger Article Five and could enmesh Washington in a war it explicitly seeks to avoid. 

Signaling force drawdowns from Europe only whets Putin’s insatiable appetite. He might assume that with the U.S. focusing on its military operation in the Middle East, now would be the best time to test NATO.

Washington must deter, not encourage, the dictator in the Kremlin. Perhaps it would be best to drop all talk of troop withdrawals, at least until there is at least a semblance of a settlement with Iran.

Dov S. Zakheim is a senior adviser at the Center for Strategic and International Studies and vice chairman of the board for the Foreign Policy Research Institute. He was undersecretary of Defense (comptroller) and chief financial officer for the Department of Defense from 2001 to 2004 and a deputy undersecretary of Defense from 1985 to 1987.

data.title: Trump trolls Jill Biden over debate 'stroke' remark, offers alternate theory
data.description:
President Trump mocked former first lady Jill Biden on social media Friday after she revealed in an interview that she believed her husband was “having a stroke” during his disastrous CNN debate performance against Trump in 2024.

“She said that she thought he was having a ‘stroke,’ and various other really bad things, and yet never rushed onto the stage to help her troubled husband, as any good wife would do,” the president wrote on Truth Social. “The only thing she failed to mention was how well I was doing prior to his near total collapse.”

“In other words, as many have asked, did my strong performance in that debate cause him to plain and simple ‘choke,’ leading to his ignominious defeat, or were other reasons the cause?” the president wrote in his post. “Nobody else knows the answer to that, BUT I DO!!!”

In a clip from an interview with CBS News released Wednesday, Jill Biden said she was “horrified” on the night of the debate as she watched her husband on stage in Atlanta.

“I was frightened, because I had never ever seen Joe like that before or since, never,” she said. “I mean, as I watched it, I thought, ‘Oh my god, he’s having a stroke.’”

Former President Biden’s famously poor debate performance sparked widespread concern about his age and health, and ultimately led to him dropping his reelection bid.

Trump has repeatedly revisited Biden’s debate performance and other gaffs, and has accused Biden’s inner circle and the media of concealing his declining condition while he was in office.

Several Democrats and prominent journalists have since come out to reveal concerns they had about Biden’s health while in office, and the subsequent push by Democrats to take him off the ballot in 2024 and replace him with former Vice President Harris.

The Bidens have denied accusations of covering up the former president’s mental acuity during his time in office.

Jill Biden has a new memoir she is promoting titled “View from the East Wing.”

data.title: Freedom 250 spokesperson: Nothing 'partisan' about fair, as musical artists drop out
data.description:
A spokesperson for Freedom 250 — the massive bash taking place on the National Mall in Washington to celebrate America’s 250th birthday — has pushed back against claims that the event is politically affiliated after a number of scheduled artists dropped out, citing complaints that they were misled.

“It is inherently nonpolitical. It is a celebration of our country,” said Freedom 250 spokesperson Julia Friedland in an interview with “Sunrise on The Hill.”

Friedland said she could not speak to the exact negotiations and conversations between event organizers and artists, but maintained that the event has always been sold as a nonpartisan celebration of the country’s 250th birthday.

“I don’t think there’s anything partisan about America being around for 250 years,” she said, adding that the event is intended to celebrate American independence and is open to all Americans.

Multiple artists who initially agreed to perform, including rocker Bret Michaels and country singer Martina McBride, publicly distanced themselves from the event after receiving backlash for their involvement. The artists also claim to have been misled about the event’s political ties.

“I was presented with an opportunity to perform at a nonpartisan event but that turned out to be misleading,” McBride wrote in an Instagram post Thursday evening. “I asked lots of questions and was assured this was a nonpartisan event that was meant to celebrate ALL 50 states.”

Rappers Vanilla Ice and Flo Rida are still expected to perform.

The festival is one of several initiatives tied to President Trump’s broader vision to highlight the country’s 250th birthday on July 4.

Freedom 250 is part of a 16-day event called the “Great American State Fair” taking place in Washington from June 25 to July 10. 

Organizers say there will be a 110-foot ferris wheel borrowed from the Smithsonian at the fair and there will be a variety of family-friendly activities as well as representatives from all 50 states and territories in attendance.

data.title: Judge temporarily blocks Justice Department's $1.8B 'anti-weaponization' fund
data.description:
A federal judge temporarily blocked the government from doling out money from President Trump’s new “anti-weaponization” fund on Friday. 

U.S. District Judge Leonic Brinkema indicated the pause is to ensure that no funds are “irreversibly disbursed” from the fund until she can rule on an emergency motion to block it. 

“It is important that the status quo be maintained until plaintiffs’ pending Motion has been resolved,” Brinkema wrote. 

Brinkema is an appointee of former President Clinton who serves on the federal bench in Alexandria, Va. 

Various groups have challenged the Justice Department’s new $1.766 billion “anti-weaponization” fund, which was created as part of a settlement after Trump sued the IRS. The fund will give payouts and issue “formal apologies” to those who pursue settlements because they believe they were wronged by the government. 

As it leaves some lawmakers fuming, the plan has come under several legal challenges. 

Brinkema is hearing a lawsuit brought by a former federal prosecutor who worked on Jan. 6 cases, a California professor arrested at an immigration protest, the city of New Haven, Conn., the National Abortion Federation and Common Cause. 

The judge will hold a June 12 hearing on their emergency motion to block the fund for the duration of the case. 

Friday’s order is temporary and landed before the government responded to the plaintiffs’ arguments. The judge ordered the Justice Department to submit its brief within the next week.

data.title: States that don't want Black voters don't deserve Black athletes
data.description:
Black athletes generate nine figures a year for colleges in states that won’t count their mothers’ votes. On Tuesday, the NAACP told them to walk. 

The NAACP’s “Out of Bounds” campaign covers eight states and 13 flagship programs in Alabama, Florida, Georgia, Louisiana, Mississippi, South Carolina, Tennessee and Texas. 

“Black athletes should not be asked to generate wealth, prestige, and power for state institutions while those same states strip political power from Black communities,” NAACP President Derrick Johnson said. “It is a sprint to erase Black political power.” 

A sprint. Not drift or erosion: a sprint. 

The Voting Rights Act is dead. Shelby County v. Holder gutted Section 5 in 2013. Brnovich v. Democratic National Committee narrowed Section 2 in 2021. Louisiana v. Callais finished the job on April 29. Justice Kagan wrote the eulogy from the dissent. The Voting Rights Act, she said, is “all but a dead letter.” 

Florida passed its redistricting bill the day Callais came down. Tennessee erased its only majority-Black district. Alabama raced to the Supreme Court to throw out its court-drawn map mid-primary. Louisiana suspended its May 16 primary so the legislature could redraw maps before a vote was counted.

Berkeley Law professor Erwin Chemerinsky did the math: It’s the largest decrease in Black representation in Congress and state legislatures since Reconstruction. 

Republican consultant Lee Atwater put the party’s strategy on tape in 1981. By 1968, you couldn’t say certain things out loud. So, you say forced busing, or states’ rights, or tax cuts. Get abstract enough, he said, and the byproduct is Black people get hurt worse than whites and nobody can prove you meant it. That is the operating manual of the modern Republican Party, dictated by the man who built it. 

If you pull up the NCAA Southeastern Conference (SEC) map, you’ll see what amounts to a hostage map. Republican Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp (R) signed SB 202 and made it a misdemeanor to hand a voter water in line. In Texas, Gov. Greg Abbott (R) signed SB 1 and banned drive-thru voting in Harris County. Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis signed SB 90 and gutted mail-in drop boxes. 

Every signature landed in a state whose flagship college football program books tens of millions in annual revenue on the labor of Black 18-year-olds. Saturday’s heroes are Tuesday’s ghosts. 

“States cannot celebrate Black excellence on Saturday while silencing Black communities on Tuesday at the ballot box,” said NAACP youth director Tylik McMillan. 

What is the point of getting money for selling your name, image or likeness — dubbed NIL deals by the NCAA — if you cannot vote? 

In December 1955, in Alabama, Black riders were the majority of the Montgomery City Lines’ fares. They walked for 381 days. By November 1956, Browder v. Gayle came down and ended segregated bussing. Not because the court grew a conscience: because the city was bleeding. 

Now the SEC. Black athletes are 40 percent of its football rosters. Division I men’s basketball is nearly half Black. The House v. NCAA settlement opened a $20.5 million annual revenue-sharing pool per program for NIL deals. Top deals cross seven figures. The transfer portal made the contract month-to-month. 

The Black rider in 1955 had to walk. The Black athlete in 2026 just has to sign somewhere else. 

On Monday, the Congressional Black Caucus sent letters to commissioners Greg Sankey at the SEC, Jim Phillips at the Atlantic Coast Conference and Charlie Baker at the NCAA. The caucus will oppose the SCORE Act — which would partially shield the NCAA from liability associated with NIL deals — until the commissioners oppose the redistricting happening inside the states their conferences sit in. 

Here is the move: Black-led NIL collectives, capitalized by Black donors, Roc Nation, the Divine Nine historically Black Greek-letter organizations and Black church networks fund deals only in states that recognize voting rights. States like Maryland, Illinois, Michigan, Oregon, New Jersey or California. 

Tuscaloosa loses the cornerback. Gainesville loses the wideout. Knoxville loses the point guard. The donor who funds the stadium and the donor who funds the state senator are often the same donor. He will not keep writing both checks. 

Critics will say athletes are not politicians. They said the same about Muhammad Ali in 1967, Tommie Smith and John Carlos at the 1968 Olympics and Colin Kaepernick in 2016. They were wrong then. They are wrong now. 

An athlete is a worker. A worker has the right to choose an employer. A Black worker in 2026 has the right to factor whether the state taxing their labor will count their vote. 

Reconstruction did not die because Black men stopped voting. It died because Black men were stripped of the economic autonomy the vote rests on. The Compromise of 1877 that ended it was about money before it was about ballots. This generation does not have to take that deal. 

Montgomery proved the case when the buses ran empty. The SEC will prove it when the rosters do. Who counts? Keep asking until Kemp, Abbott and DeSantis answer. Until Sankey loses a five-star recruit. 

Make them choose. 

Michael Starr Hopkins is the author of “Burn the Playbook,” a former senior congressional aide and presidential campaign spokesman.

data.title: Comer takes heat over arrangements for Bondi interview on Epstein files
data.description:
House Oversight Committee leadership clashed over the terms of former Attorney General Pam Bondi’s closed door appearance Friday, with Democrats bashing Chair James Comer (R-Ky.) for agreeing to a voluntary interview.

Bondi was subpoenaed for a deposition in March, but Comer agreed to let Bondi sit for a transcribed interview, a different format in which subjects are not sworn in and the interview is not video taped.

Speaking with reporters Friday before the start of the interview, Comer described Bondi as coming in “voluntarily” for a second time, noting she also organized a prior closed door meeting with the panel that Democrats had described as an effort to sidestep the subpoena.

“She came a few months ago for a briefing. Unfortunately, the Democrats got up and walked out of that briefing. Many of the Republicans asked her questions for a couple of hours during that briefing, and I’m appreciative that she’s coming back today voluntarily for a transcribed interview,” Comer said.

Ranking member Robert Garcia (D-Calif.) criticized Comer for a format where Bondi would appear without being videotaped.

“I just want to be very clear that we continue to be incredibly disappointed of the decision to not have this interview videotaped and then released to the American public. The second thing we asked Chairman Comer is to ensure that this interview was under oath. We understand the Attorney General is going to be there answering questions from Congress, but it should have been under oath and it should be videotaped,” he said.

Comer stressed that it’s a crime to lie to Congress whether under oath or not, but during his press conference he was pressed to dialogue with a group of Epstein survivors who said the meeting should be videotaped.

“If there are questions that aren’t asked, anything you’re not satisfied, let us know, and we’ll do everything in our ability to get answers for you all,” he said to the group.

After Comer left, the survivors repeated that they wanted to see a taped interview.

“I do want to make a note also about the importance of body language and inflection in the voice. There’s something called subtext that we all know about, and when we get a transcribed interview, we have no idea. It’ could be totally lost in translation, so it’s incredibly vital that we see filmed interviews, not just the transcription under oath,” said Dani Bensky.

Lauren Hersh, CEO of World Without Exploitation, who accompanied the survivors, called the transcribed interviews “unacceptable.” 

“We need to see body language. We want to see the pauses. We know that these witnesses can go off record and things transpire off the record that the American people are not able to see, and we think it is critically important in this moment,” she said.

“Chairman Comer is exactly right. These women have been failed by numerous administrations, that is true, but this is the moment where we need truth, transparency, and ultimately accountability.”

data.title: Facing a threat to their privilege, Democrats call on Black men to sacrifice their careers
data.description:
Somewhere in Georgia right now, there’s a 17-year-old who has spent the last decade working toward one thing. He is the first in his family with a realistic shot at a college education paid in full. His coaches know his name. College recruiters have his number.

He has not been asked whether he wants to be a political instrument, but the NAACP just decided he will be.

The organization’s new “Out of Bounds” campaign calls on Black student-athletes to boycott Southeastern Conference schools in Alabama, Florida, Georgia, Louisiana, Mississippi, South Carolina, Tennessee, and Texas. The trigger is the Supreme Court’s ruling in Louisiana v. Callais, which reinterpreted Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act and cleared the way for states to redraw majority-Black congressional districts. NAACP President Derrick Johnson argues that institutions profiting from Black athletic talent cannot stay silent while states dismantle Black political representation.

The campaign’s economic leverage rests on football and basketball — the sports that generate the bulk of SEC revenue, with rosters overwhelmingly composed of Black men.

What the NAACP frames as a voting rights crisis is more accurately a Democratic Party infrastructure problem. No Black voter lost the right to cast a ballot in Louisiana v. Callais. What changed is whether states must draw districts around racial composition to produce reliably Democratic seats.

Majority-minority maps have long functioned as structural guarantees for Democratic incumbents — Black voters are packed into engineered districts, and this is called “representation.”

When those maps get redrawn, the casualty will not be Black voting power. The casualty will be the Democratic Party’s structural advantage. That is what the boycott is protecting. Young Black athletes are being asked to sacrifice themselves on the altar of a political party’s special privilege.

The athletes this campaign targets are young men in their late teens. They have spent their lives building toward this moment: the scholarship, the name, image and likeness deal, the NFL or NBA draft. The decision to turn down what is arguably college football’s strongest conference could be the difference between a career and a detour.

College transfer portals — through which athletes move between schools — remain closed until 2027. The NAACP campaign’s practical impact on current rosters is therefore zero. The only real cost falls on high school recruits, who are being asked to gamble their futures on a political campaign whose outcome they cannot control, and from which they may not benefit.

These young Black men generate more than $1 billion annually for the very institutions the NAACP targets. In return, they are asked to walk away from an education, from NIL earnings and from their futures. The NAACP risks nothing — its executives keep their salaries, its donors keep their relationships. The cost falls entirely on those whose labor built the revenue.

That is not solidarity. It is a business model.

And it gets worse. The NAACP and the Congressional Black Caucus coordinated this campaign, starting with a joint press conference. The same week, House Democrats announced united opposition to the Student Compensation and Opportunity Through Rights and Endorsements Act — legislation that would have set national standards for athlete compensation.

Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) pulled the bill before a vote was held because two of the original Democratic co-sponsors — Reps. Shomari Figures (D-Ala.) and Janelle Bynum (D-Ore.) — had withdrawn their support.

The bill that could have put money in these young men’s pockets fell to the same political fight they are now being asked to fight. They lost twice. The people who cost them lost nothing.

The selectivity is also worth naming. This campaign is built on Black men’s labor. Yet the Black women who lead prominent programs at these same institutions face no such demands to sacrifice themselves. Dawn Staley, the most celebrated women’s basketball coach in America,  leads South Carolina’s program. Yolett McPhee-McCuin coaches at Ole Miss. Joni Taylor at Texas A&M. None of them have been asked to walk away.

In the NAACP’s playbook, the conversation about sacrifice centers on Black men.

This pattern is not new. In 2021, the NAACP urged professional athletes to avoid signing with Texas teams over voting laws. In 2024, it called on Black athletes to reconsider Florida schools over diversity, equity and inclusion policies. Neither campaign had any visible effect on recruiting. And each time, the institutional class watches from the sideline.

Real pressure doesn’t require a young Black man to sacrifice his future. The Black adults who have spent decades building careers and influence aligned with the Democratic Party have the power to apply it. Choosing silence while calling on teenagers to bear the costs is simply a failure of leadership.

Whenever and however the redistricting fight is won, it won’t be because a 17-year-old in Georgia listened to some terrible advice from professional activists and sacrificed his future. It will be because the people with actual power finally used it.

Real solidarity doesn’t send the youngest people in the room to pay the tab for the oldest.

Melik Abdul is a D.C.-based public affairs professional and Republican strategist.
